title: Illinois releases AI guidance for public schools ahead of new school year
subtitle: The state's education agency issued a framework of examples and cautions for districts weighing how to use AI in classrooms — non-binding but the reference document local boards will now cite.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Illinois education officials on July 14 released statewide guidance on the use of artificial intelligence in public schools, publishing a framework of examples and cautions intended to help districts decide whether and how to incorporate the technology. The document is advisory rather than binding — districts still set their own policies — but it now becomes the reference school boards, superintendents, and unions will cite when drafting local rules. It arrives roughly six weeks before the start of the 2026-27 school year, timed so districts can adopt policies before students return.

The guidance follows Illinois Senate Bill 315 — the AI Safety Measures Act signed on July 7 — but covers a different layer. SB315 governs high-risk deployment of AI systems by the state; the classroom guidance covers what teachers and students should and should not do with commercial tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and Copilot. The distinction matters because it is where most of the day-to-day judgment calls actually happen: whether a student using an AI tutor is 'using' or 'cheating,' whether a teacher grading with AI has to disclose it, and how student work fed into a commercial model is protected.

Illinois is one of the last large states to publish this kind of guidance — California, Texas, Ohio, Virginia, and New York shipped versions in the last twelve months, and the National Governors Association has been pushing for state coordination. Each state's document reads similarly on paper (encourage AI literacy, protect student data, disclose AI use in graded work) but the enforcement teeth vary widely. In practice, the guidance that a superintendent's lawyers will actually apply is the one written by their own state — which is why the July 14 release matters more inside Illinois than the same paragraphs would in the federal register.

A note for learners: if you are a teacher, a parent, or a student in Illinois, the practical question this week is whether your district has now written a policy that references the new guidance. Ask your school board. States publish frameworks; districts turn them into rules; and the rules that will govern your classroom in September are being drafted right now — often by three people in a summer meeting with no public input.

sources.title: Illinois education officials release guidance on use of AI in public schools — ABC7 Chicago
